title: Why do tree-based models still outperform deep learning on tabular data?
authors: Léo Grinsztajn, Edouard Oyallon, Gaël Varoquaux (Soda, Inria Saclay; ISIR, CNRS, Sorbonne University)
tokenization_method: This is a benchmark + inductive-bias study, not a tokenizer proposal. It evaluates other works' tokenizers: FT-Transformer's per-feature affine token (scalar*W+bias for numerical, lookup for categorical) and SAINT's embedding module + inter-sample attention. Its own preprocessing 'tokenization' is deliberately minimal: numerical features are Gaussianized via scikit-learn QuantileTransformer; categorical features are OneHotEncoded for models that do not handle them natively (and GBT variants get native categorical support via HistGradientBoosting). The paper's headline tokenization-relevant claim: ANY per-feature embedding layer (periodic, target-aware binning, or even a generic learned embedding) helps NNs primarily because it BREAKS the rotation invariance of MLP-style learners and injects capacity for high-frequency / irregular target functions.
feature_types_handled: numerical and low-cardinality categorical only. HARD SCOPE LIMITS imposed by the benchmark's preprocessing: categorical features with >20 distinct items are DROPPED (no high-cardinality categoricals); all missing data is removed (columns then rows); time-series / stream data is excluded (i.i.d. only, no temporal); datasets truncated to ~10K (medium) or studied at 50K (large) rows; high-dimensional (d/n > 1/10) datasets excluded. So: numerical + bounded-cardinality categorical, static, complete-case, medium-sized.
token_granularity: n/a for its own contribution. For the DL models it benchmarks, granularity is per-feature (FT-Transformer / SAINT one token per feature). The study's findings (rotation non-invariance, irregular target functions, uninformative-feature robustness) are about how the FEATURE BASIS is represented, which is upstream of and motivates per-feature (vs rotation-mixed) tokenization.
numerical_strategy: For its benchmark pipeline: Gaussianize via QuantileTransformer, then feed raw transformed scalars to NNs (or per-feature scalar*vector token inside FT-Transformer). The study's Finding 1 (NNs are biased to overly-smooth, low-frequency solutions and fail on irregular target functions) is its key statement ABOUT numerical encoding: it explicitly hypothesizes that periodic embeddings (Gorishniy et al. 2022) help by adding high-frequency capacity and that target-aware binning helps by making the target function smoother for the NN.
categorical_strategy: OneHotEncoder for non-native models; native categorical handling for HistGradientBoosting/CatBoost-style trees. No learned entity embeddings in its own pipeline beyond what FT-Transformer/SAINT bring. The paper finds (Sec 4.2) that categorical variables are NOT the main weakness of NNs: the NN-vs-tree gap is narrower on numerical-only data but most of the gap persists even with numerical features alone.
high_cardinality_handling: NONE — explicitly excluded. Categorical features with more than 20 unique items are removed during dataset preprocessing. The Discussion (Sec 6) lists 'the best way to handle high-cardinality categorical features' as an explicit OPEN QUESTION the study does not answer. This is a critical gap for the 70+-heterogeneous-feature target setting which is dominated by high-cardinality fields.
temporal_handling: NONE — explicitly excluded. Stream-like datasets and time series are removed by the 'I.I.D. data' inclusion criterion. The study has no temporal or temporal-static-fusion content.
scales_to_many_features: Not directly addressed; the benchmark excludes high-dimensional datasets (d/n > 1/10) and focuses on medium-sized data. However Finding 2 + Finding 3 are HIGHLY relevant to the scaling-to-many-features problem: tabular datasets contain many uninformative features (a GBT loses little accuracy after removing up to ~50% of features ranked by importance), and MLP-style rotation-invariant learners have worst-case sample complexity that grows at least LINEARLY in the number of irrelevant features (citing Ng 2004). So adding more (often uninformative) features disproportionately hurts MLPs vs trees/FT-Transformer. Implication: at 70+ features, feature selection / non-rotation-invariant per-feature representation matters more, not less.
downstream_model: Benchmarks tree-based models (scikit-learn RandomForest, GradientBoostingTrees/HistGradientBoosting, XGBoost) against deep models (MLP and ResNet from Gorishniy et al. 2021; FT-Transformer as the DL 'best case'; SAINT for inter-sample attention). No new model is proposed.
pretraining_objective: none. All models trained supervised from scratch per dataset. No self-supervised / transfer objective is studied.
key_results: On 45 curated OpenML datasets across 4 settings (medium ~10K / large ~50K rows x with/without categorical features), with a ~400-iteration random hyperparameter search per model per dataset (15-30 reshuffles for bootstrap-like variance estimates; ~20,000 total compute-hours, results released): tree-based models (esp. XGBoost / GBT) remain state-of-the-art and beat all NNs (MLP, ResNet, FT-Transformer, SAINT) at EVERY random-search budget; the gap stays wide even after extensive tuning, and does not even count trees' much faster training. FT-Transformer is the strongest DL model and the 'best case' for DL but still loses. Three inductive-bias findings explain the gap: (1) NNs are biased toward overly-smooth, low-frequency solutions and fail on irregular target functions (Gaussian-kernel-smoothing the target hurts trees a lot but barely hurts NNs -> the real targets are irregular and NNs cannot fit them); (2) NNs (especially MLP/ResNet) are not robust to uninformative features (removing low-importance features narrows the NN-tree gap; adding uninformative Gaussian features widens it); (3) data are not rotationally invariant so learners should not be either: ResNet is empirically rotation-invariant and random rotations REVERSE the ranking (NNs then beat trees, ResNet beats FT-Transformer), showing the natural per-feature basis carries information that rotation-invariant learners destroy. Categorical variables are NOT the main NN weakness; the gap mostly persists on numerical-only data.
baselines_compared: Tree-based: scikit-learn RandomForest, GradientBoostingTrees / HistGradientBoosting, XGBoost. Deep: MLP, ResNet (both from Gorishniy et al. 2021), FT-Transformer (Gorishniy et al. 2021), SAINT (Somepalli et al. 2021). Default-hyperparameter Logistic/Linear Regression used only as a 'too-easy dataset' filter.
transferable_idea: The 'break-rotation-invariance' lens on per-feature embeddings: a numerical/categorical embedding layer helps deep tabular models LESS because of representational richness per se and MORE because (a) it breaks the rotation invariance of MLP-style learners (preserving the meaningful per-feature basis, which matters more as uninformative features accumulate) and (b) it can inject high-frequency capacity (periodic embeddings) or smooth the effective target (target-aware binning). For a 70+-heterogeneous-feature tokenizer this argues for: keep tokens per-feature (never mix features by a learned linear projection over the whole vector before the model can isolate them), prefer numerical embeddings that add high-frequency capacity (PLR/periodic) over raw scalars, and pair tokenization with explicit feature selection/gating because uninformative features hurt deep models super-linearly.
limitations: Authors-stated open questions: which OTHER tree inductive biases matter; behavior on very small or very large datasets; the right handling of missing data and high-cardinality categoricals (all excluded here); how conclusions change once those are handled. Methodological scope: medium-sized (~10K) regime is the headline; binary classification only for the inductive-bias experiments (Sec 5 restricts to numerical features + classification); single-target-per-dataset OpenML curation; the rotation-invariance argument leans on Ng (2004) worst-case theory plus an empirical rotation test, not a per-component ablation.
pitfalls: 1) SCOPE MISMATCH for this topic: the benchmark explicitly removes high-cardinality categoricals (>20 items), all missing data, and all temporal/stream data — exactly the three things the 70+-mixed-feature target setting is full of. Do NOT cite 'trees still win' as a verdict on the high-cardinality/temporal setting; the study never tested it (it lists those as open questions). 2) 'Trees beat DL' is conditioned on medium-sized (~10K) data; the paper itself flags the large-data regime as under-studied and other work shows the gap narrows or flips with more data / pretraining (TabPFN-style, large-scale). 3) The claim 'embeddings help because they break rotation invariance' is the authors' INTERPRETATION linking their rotation experiment to Gorishniy 2022 / SAINT — it is not an ablation that toggles the embedding while holding rotation fixed, so treat it as a strong hypothesis, not a proven causal mechanism. 4) 'Categorical variables are not the main weakness of NNs' is measured on LOW-cardinality (<=20) one-hot categoricals only; it says nothing about high-cardinality fields, which is precisely where naive one-hot/embedding tokenization is known to break.
relevance_to_topic: It is the canonical evidence base for WHY deep tabular models lag and therefore WHY tokenization design matters: per-feature (rotation-invariance-breaking) tokens, high-frequency numerical embeddings, and feature-selection-aware tokenization are the levers it implicates — but it explicitly excludes high-cardinality, missing, and temporal features, so it sets the motivation and the rules of the game without covering the topic's hardest cases.
code_available: yes — official benchmark code + the full 20,000-hour random-search raw results at https://github.com/LeoGrin/tabular-benchmark; datasets shared via OpenML.
